title: Hormones and the Endocrine System Quiz

prompt.text: In the context of the endocrine system, what is the best definition of a hormone?

options: An electrical impulse that travels along a neuron to trigger a muscle contraction.
A chemical signaling molecule secreted into the circulatory fluid that communicates regulatory messages throughout the body.
A digestive enzyme secreted into the small intestine to break down complex starches.
A specialized structural protein that provides elasticity to the walls of large arteries.
questions.answer: A chemical signaling molecule secreted into the circulatory fluid that communicates regulatory messages throughout the body.

prompt.text: Which of the following is NOT a major class of hormones based on their chemical structure?

options: Peptide hormones
Steroid hormones
Amine hormones
Carbohydrate hormones
questions.answer: Carbohydrate hormones

prompt.text: Which of the following is an example of a steroid hormone?

options: Insulin
Cortisol
Epinephrine
Thyroxine
questions.answer: Cortisol

prompt.text: Which of the following is an example of a polypeptide hormone?

options: Insulin
Cortisol
Epinephrine
Thyroxine
questions.answer: Insulin

prompt.text: Which of the following is an example of a amine hormone?

options: Insulin
Cortisol
Epinephrine
Thyroxine
questions.answer: Epinephrine

prompt.text: The endocrine system primarily regulates body functions through:

options: Electrical signals transmitted by neurons.
Chemical signals secreted into the bloodstream.
Mechanical signals generated by muscle contractions.
Thermal signals produced by metabolic activity.
questions.answer: Chemical signals secreted into the bloodstream.

prompt.text: What is the primary difference in how the endocrine system and the nervous system coordinate body functions?

options: The endocrine system uses rapid electrical signals for short-term changes, while the nervous system uses slow-moving chemicals for long-term growth.
The endocrine system sends chemical signals through the bloodstream for slow, long-lasting effects, while the nervous system uses electrical impulses for fast, immediate responses.
The endocrine system only controls the brain, while the nervous system controls every other organ in the body.
The endocrine system signals travel only to neighboring cells, while nervous system signals travel exclusively through the blood.
questions.answer: The endocrine system sends chemical signals through the bloodstream for slow, long-lasting effects, while the nervous system uses electrical impulses for fast, immediate responses.

prompt.text: Endocrine Signaling

categories: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.
Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.
questions.answer: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.

prompt.text: Paracrine Signaling

categories: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.
Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.
questions.answer: Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.

prompt.text: Autocrine Signaling

categories: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.
Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.
questions.answer: Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.

prompt.text: Synaptic Signaling

categories: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.
Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.
questions.answer: Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.

prompt.text: Neuroendocrine Signaling

categories: Molecules diffuse into the bloodstream and trigger responses in target cells anywhere in the body.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in neighboring cells.
Molecules diffuse locally and trigger a response in the same cell that secreted them.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across a synapse and trigger a response in cells of target tissues.
Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.
questions.answer: Neurohormones diffuse from nerve endings into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.

prompt.text: What is the primary difference between a hormone and a pheromone?

options: Hormones are used for internal regulation within an individual, while pheromones are chemical signals released into the environment to communicate with other individuals of the same species.
Hormones only affect the nervous system, while pheromones only affect the digestive system.
Hormones are always proteins, while pheromones are always made of pure oxygen gas.
Hormones travel through the air to reach target cells, while pheromones travel exclusively through the bloodstream.
questions.answer: Hormones are used for internal regulation within an individual, while pheromones are chemical signals released into the environment to communicate with other individuals of the same species.

prompt.text: What is the primary difference in how water-soluble hormones and lipid-soluble hormones interact with their target cells?

options: Water-soluble hormones can diffuse directly through the cell membrane, while lipid-soluble hormones must bind to receptors on the cell surface.
Water-soluble hormones bind to cell-surface receptors and trigger signal transduction, while lipid-soluble hormones pass through the membrane to bind with internal receptors.
Water-soluble hormones are used exclusively for brain function, while lipid-soluble hormones are used only for muscle repair.
Water-soluble hormones enter the nucleus to change DNA directly, while lipid-soluble hormones are destroyed by the cell membrane upon contact.
questions.answer: Water-soluble hormones bind to cell-surface receptors and trigger signal transduction, while lipid-soluble hormones pass through the membrane to bind with internal receptors.

prompt.text: Which brain structure serves as the primary link between the nervous system and the endocrine system, coordinating the body's hormonal regulation?

options: Cerebellum
Thalamus
Hypothalamus
Medulla oblongata
questions.answer: Hypothalamus

prompt.text: How does the hypothalamus translate nervous system stimuli into endocrine system responses?

options: It uses electrical sparks to physically jump-start the production of hormones in the blood.
It receives sensory data from the brain and responds by secreting 'releasing hormones' that trigger the anterior pituitary gland.
It converts sound waves directly into adrenaline to prepare the body for a fight-or-flight response.
It sends motor neurons directly into the pancreas to force the release of insulin.
questions.answer: It receives sensory data from the brain and responds by secreting 'releasing hormones' that trigger the anterior pituitary gland.

prompt.text: Which of the following correctly characterizes the tissue types and secretion methods of the two pituitary lobes?

options: The anterior pituitary is an endocrine gland that synthesizes and secretes hormones, while the posterior pituitary is neural tissue that secretes neurohormones produced in the hypothalamus.
The anterior pituitary is neural tissue that secretes neurohormones, while the posterior pituitary is an endocrine gland that manufactures its own hormones.
Both lobes are endocrine glands that lack any direct connection to the brain's nervous tissue.
The anterior lobe secretes only neurotransmitters, while the posterior lobe secretes only digestive enzymes.
questions.answer: The anterior pituitary is an endocrine gland that synthesizes and secretes hormones, while the posterior pituitary is neural tissue that secretes neurohormones produced in the hypothalamus.

prompt.text: Secretes thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3) to regulate the body's metabolic rate, heart function, and digestive health.

categories: Thyroid Gland
Parathyroid Gland
Testosterone
Estrogen
questions.answer: Thyroid Gland

prompt.text: Produces a hormone that acts to increase blood calcium levels by stimulating release from bones and reabsorption in the kidneys.

categories: Thyroid Gland
Parathyroid Gland
Testosterone
Estrogen
questions.answer: Parathyroid Gland

prompt.text: An androgen that promotes the development of male secondary sexual characteristics and stimulates sperm production.

categories: Thyroid Gland
Parathyroid Gland
Testosterone
Estrogen
questions.answer: Testosterone

prompt.text: Primarily responsible for the development of female secondary sexual characteristics and the maintenance of the reproductive system.

categories: Thyroid Gland
Parathyroid Gland
Testosterone
Estrogen
questions.answer: Estrogen
